schools.summary: Paul's account of his concern: 'when we could no longer forbear (stegontes — endure, contain our anxiety, hold out)' — his longing and worry for the Thessalonians grew so great that he could bear the suspense no longer; 'we thought it good (resolved) to be left at Athens alone' — he chose to send Timothy to them (v.2), even though it meant being left by himself at Athens, deprived of his companion's help, in a strange and hostile city, a real sacrifice borne for their comfort (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: when we could no longer forbear, we thought it good to be left at Athens alone
Paul's willingness to be left alone at Athens
sending Timothy at the cost of his solitude
his anxious love for them

schools.summary: The honour Paul gives Timothy: 'Timotheus, our brother, and minister of God (a servant of God in the gospel), and our fellowlabourer in the gospel of Christ' — three titles marking Timothy's worth, that the Thessalonians might receive him as a weighty messenger; sent 'to establish you (sterixai — to make firm, strengthen, confirm)' — to steady and strengthen them that their faith might not be shaken by persecution; 'and to comfort (encourage, exhort) you concerning your faith' — to encourage them amid their trials, the double purpose being to confirm and to console (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: sent Timotheus, our brother, and minister of God, and our fellowlabourer in the gospel of Christ
to establish you, and to comfort you concerning your faith
Timothy sent to establish and comfort them
strengthening their faith against persecution

schools.summary: The purpose of Timothy's mission: 'that no man should be moved (sainesthai — shaken, disturbed, swayed, beguiled) by these afflictions' — that none be unsettled or turned aside from the faith by the persecutions they endured; 'these afflictions' being the present persecutions, real and pressing trials that might tempt the weak to abandon the faith (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: 'yourselves know that we (Christians) are appointed (set, destined) thereunto' — afflictions are no strange accident but the appointed lot of believers; suffering is part of the Christian calling, foretold and ordained, so it should not surprise or shake them.

themes: That no man should be moved by these afflictions
for yourselves know that we are appointed thereunto
believers appointed to afflictions
not to be moved by persecution

schools.summary: Paul had prepared them in advance: 'when we were with you, we told you before (proelegomen — forewarned you) that we should suffer tribulation' — during his ministry among them, Paul had plainly predicted that suffering would come; 'even as it came to pass (so it happened), and ye know' — the prediction confirmed by the event, the persecution occurring exactly as foretold, the fulfilment proving he had told them the truth and meant to steady, not shake, their faith (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: when we were with you, we told you before that we should suffer tribulation
even as it came to pass, and ye know
Paul's forewarning of the tribulation
the prediction confirmed by the event

schools.summary: Paul repeats his anxious concern: 'when I could no longer forbear (contain my anxiety), I sent (Timothy) to know your faith' — to learn whether their faith stood firm under persecution; 'lest by some means the tempter (ho peirazon — Satan, the one who tempts) have tempted you' — he feared that Satan, using their afflictions, might have seduced them from the faith, persecution being the tempter's tool; 'and our labour be in vain (fruitless, lost)' — lest, if they fell, all Paul's toil among them be wasted (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

Calvin: Paul's anxiety was for the fruit of his ministry as well as for their souls; he dreaded that the tempter's assault through affliction might overthrow the faith he had planted and render his labour void.

themes: I sent to know your faith, lest by some means the tempter have tempted you
and our labour be in vain
Paul's fear lest the tempter had undone his labour
Satan tempting through affliction

schools.summary: The relief of Timothy's return: he 'brought us good tidings (euangelisamenou — brought good news, the word used of the gospel itself) of your faith and charity (love)' — the report that their faith and love stood firm being itself like good news; 'that ye have good remembrance of us always' — they remembered Paul with affection and gratitude, not turned against him by his absence or his enemies' slanders; 'desiring greatly to see us, as we also to see you' — the longing mutual, love answering love (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: Timotheus came from you unto us, and brought us good tidings of your faith and charity
desiring greatly to see us, as we also to see you
Timothy's good tidings of their faith and love
the mutual longing

schools.summary: The effect of Timothy's report on Paul: 'we were comforted (parakalethemen — encouraged, consoled) over you... by your faith' — the news of the Thessalonians' steadfast faith bringing the anxious apostle real consolation; this comfort coming 'in all our affliction (thlipsis — pressure, trouble) and distress (anagke — necessity, hardship)' — Paul himself then in the midst of his own sufferings (at Corinth), yet consoled even there specifically 'by your faith,' their steadfastness in believing despite persecution being his consolation (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: we were comforted over you in all our affliction and distress by your faith
Paul comforted in his own distress by their faith
their steadfastness his consolation
comfort amid his own trials

schools.summary: Paul's vivid words: 'for now we live, if ye stand fast (steko — stand firm, hold your ground) in the Lord' — their steadfastness was so dear to him that he speaks of it as his very life; the hyperbole of love, his anxiety having been like death to him, and now, hearing they stood firm, he 'lives,' revived and restored, his burden lifted; their welfare bound up with his own happiness, his joy depending on their perseverance in union with the Lord (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: For now we live, if ye stand fast in the Lord
Paul lives when they stand fast
their steadfastness his very life
revived by their firmness in the Lord

schools.summary: Paul's overflowing thanks: 'what thanks can we render to God again for you?' — his gratitude so great that he asks what adequate return of thanks he can possibly make to God for them, words failing his thankfulness; 'for all the joy wherewith we joy for your sakes' — the great gladness their faith and steadfastness brought him, the measure of his thankfulness; 'before our God' — a holy joy offered up in God's presence, not a merely natural gladness, Paul rejoicing over them as in the sight of God (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: what thanks can we render to God again for you
for all the joy wherewith we joy for your sakes before our God
Paul's overflowing thanksgiving for their joy
a holy joy before God

schools.summary: The fervour of Paul's prayer: 'night and day praying exceedingly (hyperekperissou — beyond all measure, super-abundantly)' — with the utmost earnestness, constantly and intensely; 'that we might see your face' — to be reunited with them in person, the desire running through the whole chapter; 'and might perfect (katartisai — complete, mend, supply, put in order) that which is lacking (the deficiencies) in your faith' — though their faith was strong, it was not yet complete, and Paul longed to come and supply what was still wanting, to build them up further (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Night and day praying exceedingly that we might see your face
and might perfect that which is lacking in your faith
Paul's earnest prayer to see them and perfect their faith
supplying what is lacking

schools.summary: The prayer addressed to both: 'now God himself and our Father, and our Lord Jesus Christ, direct our way unto you' — Father and Son named together as the one to whom Paul prays, and notably the verb that follows ('direct,' kateuthynai — make straight, guide, clear the road of obstacles) is singular, the Father and the Son addressed as one; Paul asks God to remove the hindrances (Satan had blocked him, 2:18) and make a straight path for his return (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: Paul prays to the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together, with a singular verb, a witness to the unity of the Son with the Father — both addressed as the one God who governs his way and orders the course of his servants.

themes: Now God himself and our Father, and our Lord Jesus Christ, direct our way unto you
Paul's prayer that God and Christ direct his way
Father and Son addressed as one
clearing the path of obstacles

schools.summary: Paul's prayer for their growth in love: 'the Lord make you to increase and abound (pleonasai kai perisseusai — multiply and overflow) in love' — that their love might not merely continue but grow and superabound; 'one toward another (within the church, mutual brotherly love), and toward all men (even outsiders, all mankind)' — a love beginning among the brethren but extending to everyone, not confined to their own circle; 'even as we do toward you' — Paul's own overflowing love for them the measure and model (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: the Lord make you to increase and abound in love one toward another, and toward all men
even as we do toward you
Paul's prayer that their love increase toward all
love modelled on his own

schools.summary: The goal of their abounding love: 'to the end he may stablish (sterixai — make firm, confirm) your hearts unblameable (without fault) in holiness before God, even our Father, at the coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus Christ' — that love may issue in a settled, blameless holiness fit to stand the scrutiny of the judgment day. On 'with all his saints (hagioi — holy ones),' Barnes takes it to include both the holy angels who come with him (Matthew 25:31) and all the redeemed; Ellicott leans rather to the glorified believers (anticipating 4:14, the 'saints' seeming to be assessors in the judgment, an honour for holy men), while allowing the word might be stretched to include the holy angels (Deuteronomy 33:2; Jude 14).

Cyril (with Calvin): the coming of the Lord with his holy ones is the great hope toward which holiness presses; whether angels or glorified men attend him, the believer is to be established blameless to meet him in that day.

debates.question: Who are 'all his saints' who come with the Lord?

sides.position: Both the holy angels (who attend Christ, Matthew 25:31) and all the redeemed
Chiefly the glorified believers (anticipating 4:14), as assessors in the judgment

themes: he may stablish your hearts unblameable in holiness before God
at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ with all his saints
unblameable holiness at Christ's coming
the crux of all his saints
